old_title: Andy Burnham latest: Trump claims incoming PM will 'open North Sea oil all the way'
new_title: Andy Burnham latest: New prime minister to be appointed by King Charles within hours
old_text:
Donald Trump has claimed that Andy Burnham "will be opening up, all the way, the invaluable North Sea Oil" and move the "horrible looking windmills".
The celebratory post on TruthSocial claimed that the people of Scotland were "dancing in the streets" over the incumbent prime minister's alleged position change on the North Sea, which is yet to be confirmed.
His comments came just hours ahead of Mr Burnham's first day in Downing Street. The former Manchester mayor is expected to address the nation on Monday afternoon after meeting with the King in Buckingham Palace.
Mr Burnham is expected to characterise the moment as one of "reflection and resolution", arguing Britain must be honest about the challenges it faces and demonstrate it can solve them.
And he will say he is "acutely conscious" that the UK has had seven prime ministers over the last 10 years, and stress the need for more stable and responsible politics.
In pictures: Larry the Cat waits outside 10 Downing Street ahead of Andy Burnham's arrival on Monday
Watch: How much do the public really know about their new PM Andy Burnham?
What's on the agenda for Monday?
Andy Burnham will become prime minister after being named Labour leader on Friday.
At 11:15am, Sir Keir Starmer is expected to give a statement in Downing Street before his visit to Buckingham Palace where he will offer his resignation to the King.
Mr Burnham will then see the King to "kiss hands" and be invited to take over as prime minister.
At 12:40pm, incoming prime minister Andy Burnham will arrive in Downing Street, where he will speak.
By 2:30pm, the House of Lords will take questions on the European Union, learning disabilities and music teachers before the third reading of the Civil Aviation Bill and report stage of the Social Housing Bill, at Westminster.
Cabinet appointments are expected to be announced from late afternoon.
Andy Burnham must deliver emergency budget to 'reverse damage', says SNP
The SNP has called on Andy Burnham to deliver an emergency cost-of-living budget to "reverse damage" under the Labour government.
The former Greater Manchester mayor, who returned to Westminster last month as the MP for Makerfield, vowed to "bring back hope" in his speech as Labour leader on Friday.
SNP Westminster leader Dave Doogan said that Mr Burnham would be "judged on his actions not words" as the UK prepares for its seventh prime minister in 10 years.
Mr Doogan is calling on Mr Burnham to deliver an emergency cost-of-living budget when he enters No 10.
He continued: "While Westminster has been stuck in constant chaos, people's lives have got worse.
"People in Scotland don't want more empty promises about 'hope in their hearts' - they want money in their pockets and meaningful change to make people's lives and livelihoods better."
He urged Mr Burnham to deliver an emergency cost of living budget, and "a meaningful package of financial support for families, to reverse the damage done under the Labour Party government and put money back into people's pockets."
Andy Burnham: 'We're not in the right place as a country'
Andy Burnham has told The Times that "we're not in the right place as a country" ahead of becoming prime minister on Monday.
Referring to his pledge that he will set out a ten-year plan for the country, Mr Burnham said: "I'm not saying that means I'm setting out to say I'm going to be here for ten years... it's more that we're not in the right place structurally, we're not in the right place as a country. I think people can feel that."
He added: "To be clear to people, it can't be done overnight. But you've got to show people where we're going, and then the steps towards it. And that's what I'm going to try to do."
Speaking about his approach to the economy, Mr Burnham said: "I think you have to rethink how we run things to get a different approach to public spending, and to running the economy - more focused on early investment, early intervention, setting people up for success, and much less paying for failure."
Referring to his plans to scrap Sir Keir Starmer's digital ID scheme, Mr Burnham added: "That's an example of what's to come, freeing up resource for something that, in my view, wouldn't make a tangible difference to people's lives anytime soon. And even if it did, it wouldn't necessarily make life easier, it might do in the long run."
new_text:
Andy Burnham will pledge to restore confidence in government and say Britain must be honest about the challenges it faces when he becomes prime minister today.
The Labour leader is expected to start appointing his cabinet on Monday afternoon after a visit to Buckingham Palace to accept the role of prime minister from the King.
Sir Keir Starmer will give a final statement in Downing Street at around 11.15am before his visit to the palace. Mr Burnham is expected to arrive outside Number 10 just before 1pm, where he will address the nation.
The former mayor of Greater Manchester is expected to launch a policy blitz this week, including scrapping Sir Keir's controversial digital ID scheme and reportedly opening the door to more oil and gas drilling in the North Sea.
US president Donald Trump reacted to reports of North Sea expansion over the weekend, saying that Mr Burnham will open oil fields "all the way". He added: "The people of Aberdeen, in Scotland, are dancing in the streets".
Analysis: Be prepared to be shocked and amazed by Burnham's cabinet picks
The one truism of reshuffles and new governments being formed is that the briefings which take place before over who gets what job are often wrong.
A lot of it is based on individuals wanting to get their names out there or supporters of a certain faction wanting their person in a specific role.
What it means is that we really know nothing until the appointment has been made. So it is quite possible that Shabana Mahmood as chancellor and Ed Miliband as foreign secretary could end up being hot air.
The fact that at least six names have been attached to the home secretary job is indicative of the guessing game going on.
But what is more interesting are the surprise names which suddenly pop up and are on nobody's radar.
Infamously in David Cameron's first reshuffle in 2012, Maria Miller walked up Downing Street and none of the journalists outside recognised her with some thinking she was a civil servant. She popped out an hour later as culture secretary.
The most predictable cabinet was Keir Starmer's in 2024 when he appointed his entire shadow cabinet to their respective roles in government. Mr Burnham's is on the opposite end of predictability so prepare to be shocked and amazed.
No loud speakers for those gathering outside Downing Street
The Metropolitan Police has said anyone gathering outside Downing Street will be banned from using loud speakers between 10.30am and 1.30pm.
It comes after pro-EU campaigner Steve Bray blasted Ode To Joy, the EU's official anthem, during Sir Keir Starmer's resignation statement outside Downing Street last month.
Analysis: Burnham's home secretary choice will be crucial - but remains a mystery
There have been a large number of briefings over who Andy Burnham will pick as his chancellor - probably home secretary Shabana Mahmood - and foreign secretary - one of the two Milibands, probably Ed - but very little on the third major office of state, home secretary.
With Ms Mahmood expected to leave the role, Mr Burnham has a huge choice to make which will tell us everything about the direction of his government. Given how important immigration, policing and culture wars are for Nigel Farage and Reform UK the decision will be crucial.
He could pick a home secretary from the party's soft left to indicate a more relaxed approach to immigration after telling the party on Friday that "we cannot out Reform Reform."
A number of names have been mentioned in this regard including former deputy PM Angela Rayner, and culture secretary Lisa Nandy.
He could continue the hardline crackdown approach of Ms Mahmood with a choice like former health secretary Wes Streeting or welfare secretary Pat McFadden.
Or he could go for a solid, steady as it goes compromise pick like former defence secretary John Healey or bringing back foreign secretary Yvette Cooper.
All these names have been linked to the role but none seem to have stuck. Yet whoever lands the job will tell us a lot about how Mr Burnham plans to deal with immigration, policing and the whole so-called culture wars agenda.
What voters really think about Burnham becoming prime minister: 'I don't think many people know what he stands for'
As Andy Burnham prepares to take office today, promising radical change and looking to move power out of London, Dan Haygarth and Jemma Robinson ask people in the capital about the incoming prime minister
Jess Phillips says she's 'still in her pyjama bottoms' and won't be walking down Downing Street today
Jess Phillips has ruled herself out of securing a ministerial position today, as she claimed she's "still got her pyjama bottoms on" hours before Andy Burnham becomes prime minister
The former safeguarding minister, who quit Sir Keir Starmer's government in May, urged Mr Burnham to place more women in positions of power as he prepares to officially move into No 10.
Asked what her position in a Burnham government could be, she told LBC: "Oh gosh, nothing. Jess Phillips has still got her pyjama bottoms on and is in Birmingham. She won't be walking down Downing Street today."
Asked whether she could get the train down this morning, she added: "One thing I do know is that there are not great rail links...I think Andy Burnham and I had one thing that we absolutely have in common - a desire not to travel on Avanti West Coast."
Media gather at Downing Street as UK prepares for seventh PM in a decade
Andy Burnham to enter No 10 promising 'breathing room' on cost of living
Andy Burnham will enter 10 Downing Street promising to give people "breathing room" against the rising cost of living.
In his first speech as prime minister, Mr Burnham will urge the country to be honest about the challenges it faces and call for "reflection and resolution".
He will also say he is "acutely conscious" that the UK has had seven prime ministers in the last decade, stressing the need for more stable and responsible politics and pledging to restore faith in government.
Allies of Mr Burnham said he will argue on Monday that political stability must deliver "tangible" improvements along with the "breathing room" on the cost of living he has previously promised.
Before the speech, Sir Keir Starmer is expected to make his own address outside No 10 before heading to Buckingham Palace to formally resign before Mr Burnham meets the King to "kiss hands" and be appointed prime minister.
Unlike Sir Keir's statement announcing his resignation, neither speech is likely to be interrupted by protesters after the police banned amplified music outside Downing Street during the handover.
After his speech, Mr Burnham is expected to name his cabinet.
Left-wing Labour MP 'happy' with whoever is chancellor - despite refusing to back Mahmood last week
Dawn Butler has insisted she is "happy" with whoever Andy Burnham decides to appoint as his chancellor, despite refusing to back Shabanna Mahmood last week.
The Labour MP, who sits on the left of the party, last week refused to back Mahmood for chancellor multiple times when asked for her opinion on the potential appointment.
But on Monday, she told Sky News: "I'm happy. No matter who Andy puts in whatever position, I will work with whoever's in that position."
She added: "We've got a Labour prime minister and Andy Burnham is going to be prime minister. I'm very happy."
Asked if it was inevitable that one side of the party would be upset with Mr Burnham's decision, she said: "I'm not quite sure one half the party will be upset because if you look at the transition from here to Andy, it has been a smooth transition."
Inside Burnham's roundabout route to No 10, according to those who know him best
Hearing from allies of incoming prime minister Andy Burnham, Dan Haygarth tracks the year that saw the mayor of Greater Manchester set his sights on Number 10.
